senses.definition: to learn how something works or how to use it

examples.x: It's a bit tricky, but you'll get the hang of parallel parking with practice.

senses.definition: to be very knowledgeable about or skillful in a particular field of work

examples.x: When it comes to astronomy, Professor Smith really knows his stuff.
The mechanic at the garage knows his stuff; he fixed my car in no time.

senses.definition: to show or teach someone how a particular job or task is done

examples.x: The mentor taught him the ropes of public speaking.

senses.definition: to have a good knowledge or understanding about how to do something or how something works

examples.x: As a seasoned chef, she really knows the ropes in the kitchen.
Before you start your new job, I'll show you around the office and make sure you know the ropes.
He's been working here for years and knows the ropes better than anyone else.
Learning to know the ropes of a new software program can take some time.
In politics, it's essential to know the ropes of diplomacy and negotiation.

senses.definition: to become acquainted with or knowledgeable about how to do a certain job or task

examples.x: During your internship, you'll have the opportunity to learn the ropes of the company and its operations.
It may take some time to learn the ropes of this software, but with practice, you'll become proficient.
He joined the team last month and is still learning the ropes of our project management system.
New recruits spend their first few weeks learning the ropes of military discipline.

senses.definition: (of a person) having extensive knowledge about or a lot of experience in a particular activity or job

examples.x: An old hand advised me that the fireman approach would suit my scrawny physique.
John is the old hand in the marketing department; he's been with the company for over 20 years.
Our coach is an old hand in the world of professional sports, with multiple championships under his belt.
Having been a chef for decades, he's considered an old hand in the culinary world.
"The old hand on the construction site offered valuable guidance to the new workers."

senses.definition: one's ability to find things of a particular type to suit one's needs

examples.x: John has a nose for trouble; he can sense when something isn't quite right.

senses.definition: something that one is exceptionally good at

examples.x: Her strong suit is her ability to analyze complex data and draw meaningful conclusions.
For this project, teamwork is your strong suit, so collaborate effectively with your colleagues.
As a chef, his strong suit is creating unique and flavorful dishes that leave diners impressed.
His strong suit in sports has always been his speed and agility.
You see, helping people in need isn't actually my strongest suit.

senses.definition: an expert in a particular field with a great deal of experience

examples.x: John is a past master at chess, and he rarely loses a game.
As a past master of diplomacy, she skillfully negotiated the international treaty.
The artist is a past master in the use of watercolors, creating stunning landscapes.
She's a past master of time management, always meeting deadlines with ease.
In the kitchen, Chef Rodriguez is a past master of blending flavors and textures.

senses.definition: to start something, such as an activity, field of study, etc., in an skillful way
to produce correct musical sounds by one's voive voice

examples.x: She has a beautiful voice and can carry a tune effortlessly.
I wish I could carry a tune like he does; he's a fantastic singer.
Unfortunately, I can't carry a tune to save my life.
The choir members can all carry a tune exceptionally well.
His ability to carry a tune made him the lead singer of the band.

senses.definition: a person who is competent in many different tasks

examples.x: John is a real jack of all trades; he can fix the plumbing, repair the roof, and even bake delicious cakes.
She's a jack of all trades in the office, handling everything from administrative tasks to marketing.
Being a jack of all trades can be useful in a small business where employees need to wear many hats.
While he may not be a master chef, he's a jack of all trades in the kitchen, cooking up a variety of dishes.

senses.definition: (of a person) with exceptional skill or talent in a particular area or field, to the point of being considered an example of excellence

examples.x: As a pianist, she is of the first water, captivating audiences worldwide with her remarkable performances.
John's culinary skills are of the first water; he's a renowned chef in the culinary world.
The engineer's innovative designs are of the first water, pushing the boundaries of technology.
Her leadership qualities are of the first water, inspiring and guiding her team to achieve great success.

senses.definition: used to describe a competent person who performs their job well and is deserving of respect and the compensation they receive

examples.x: As the team's captain, she's worth her salt; her leadership and performance on the field are exceptional.
A good manager should be worth their salt, capable of guiding the team to success.
In a crisis, a doctor who's worth their salt remains calm and makes critical decisions under pressure.
The detective proved he was worth his salt by solving the complex case in record time.
An effective teacher is worth their salt; they inspire and educate their students, shaping their future.

senses.definition: to have a clear understanding or knowledge of a situation or matter
to have a great deal of information regarding a particular subject

examples.x: When it comes to repairing vintage cars, John really knows his onions; he can fix anything under the hood.
She's the one you should consult about gardening; she knows her onions when it comes to growing beautiful flowers.
In the world of finance, Sarah knows her onions and can provide excellent investment advice.

senses.definition: to practice or engage in an activity only to maintain a skill or ability

examples.x: He works in marketing now, but he occasionally takes on design projects to keep his hand in graphic design.
Although she retired from teaching, she still volunteers at the local school to keep her hand in and stay connected with education.
Even though he's a successful businessman, he continues to play the piano to keep his hand in music.
Retired engineers often keep their hand in by mentoring young engineers or consulting on projects.

senses.definition: a set of techniques that are only shared among members of a particular profession

examples.x: Journalists have to learn the tricks of the trade quickly if they want to get the good stories.
Magazines often improve photographs before they print them - it's one of the tricks of the trade.
There are a few tricks of the trade which will help you when painting a room.
We asked five successful writers to reveal some of the tricks of the trade.

senses.definition: to have the ability to play music by listening to it and recreating the sounds and melodies without relying on sheet music or written notation

examples.x: He's a talented pianist who can play by ear and entertain any crowd.
In improvisational theater, actors often have to play by ear and respond to audience suggestions.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv vˈæn ɡˈɑːɡz ˈɪɹ fɔːɹ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to be exceptionally talented and naturally skilled in a particular area

word_list.pronunciation: ɡɛt hæv ðə bˈeɪsɪks dˌaʊn stɹˈeɪt ɹˈaɪt

senses.definition: to have the fundamental knowledge or skills that is required

examples.x: Before you even thinking about using software to make your program more complex, you need to make sure you have the basics straight.
First, let's get the basics straight with a quick look at data generated by educational institutions.
If you can't get the basics right, it doesn't matter how brilliant your strategy is.
Once I had the basics down, it was all about practice.
Once you have the basics right, hammock camping is easy as well as safe and fun.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv ɐ wˈeɪ wɪð ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to have a natural talent in dealing with a particular type of people or things

examples.x: He has a way with words that makes his speeches captivating.
The chef has a way with spices that creates incredibly flavorful dishes.
John has a way with negotiations; he can always find a compromise.
She has a way with animals and can calm even the most skittish of pets.
